moveAnnotations.annotation: The Kádas Opening is an unconventional first move that immediately breaks from central control—we're staking a claim on the kingside flank instead of competing for d4 and e4. By pushing the h-pawn, we prepare to potentially support a kingside expansion (like g4) while keeping our intentions mysterious and avoiding heavily analyzed main lines. This move is rarely seen at the highest levels, but it can be effective against unprepared opponents who may overextend themselves while we build an unexpected attacking setup.

Black mirrors your flank strategy, immediately claiming space on the queenside and preparing to support potential pawn advances like a4. By playing symmetrically, Black prevents you from gaining any immediate space advantage and signals that they won't allow you a free hand on the kingside—both sides are now playing for flank control rather than the center. This move keeps the position balanced and unorthodox, which is exactly what Black should do against such an unusual opening.
